content.text: Team-level cost reviews translate company-wide FinOps data into actionable engineering decisions. Unlike executive dashboards that show aggregates, team reviews focus on the specific workloads, services, and code changes that drive a team's AI spend — and they produce concrete action items with owners and deadlines.

attrs.alt: Team cost review meeting cadence and action funnel

content.text: Effective cost governance uses four distinct meeting cadences, each with a different scope, depth, and audience. The key discipline is keeping each cadence true to its purpose — weekly check-ins that turn into 90-minute forensics sessions crowd out real work without improving outcomes.

The monthly review is the workhorse of team-level cost governance. A tight agenda with pre-read materials distributed 48 hours in advance ensures the meeting produces decisions rather than data presentations.
Pre-read review (5 min): Confirm all attendees have read the cost report sent 48 hours prior — defer meeting if pre-read completion is below 80%.
Metrics summary (5 min): FinOps analyst walks through MTD spend, unit cost trend, and budget status. No raw numbers — only direction and magnitude.
Anomaly discussion (10 min): Each flagged anomaly gets a 2-minute slot: what happened, what the team already did, and whether further action is needed.
Action item review (10 min): Review previous month's action items. Mark complete, escalate blocked items, close items whose impact was neutral.
New action items (10 min): Identify and assign new action items using the Identify → Assign → Act → Verify funnel. Every item leaves with an owner, deadline, and success metric.
Forecast check (5 min): Is the team on track for monthly budget? If forecast exceeds budget by more than 5%, discuss whether to apply throttles or request supplemental budget.

Vague action items die between meetings. Every action item produced in a cost review must have four fields: owner (a named person, not a team), due date (a specific date, not 'next sprint'), success metric (quantified threshold), and a ticket ID in the team's project tracker.

The pre-read report is sent to all meeting attendees 48 hours before the monthly review. It is a structured one-page document (or Notion/Confluence page) that attendees should be able to read in under 10 minutes.
One sentence: status green/amber/red and why
4–6 metrics with current value and trend vs prior month
Each anomaly: what, when, magnitude, current status
Each open item: status, outcome if closed, blocker if blocked

def generate_pre_read_report(
    team: str,
    kpis: list[dict],
    anomalies: list[str],
    open_actions: list[CostActionItem],
    meeting_date: datetime.date,
) -> str:
    """Generate a plain-text pre-read report for the monthly cost review."""
    lines = [
        f"=== {team} — Monthly AI Cost Review Pre-Read ===",
        f"Meeting: {meeting_date.strftime('%B %d, %Y')}",
        "",
        "--- KEY METRICS ---",
    ]
    for kpi in kpis:
        lines.append(f"  {kpi['label']:<30} {kpi['value']:>12}   {kpi['delta']:>8} vs last month")
    lines += ["", "--- ANOMALIES ---"]
    for a in anomalies:
        lines.append(f"  • {a}")
    lines += ["", "--- OPEN ACTION ITEMS ---"]
    for item in open_actions:
        lines.append(f"  {item.format_summary()}")
    lines += ["", "--- AGENDA ---",
              "  1. Pre-read confirmation (5 min)",
              "  2. Metrics summary (5 min)",
              "  3. Anomaly discussion (10 min)",
              "  4. Action item review (10 min)",
              "  5. New action items (10 min)",
              "  6. Forecast check (5 min)"]
    return "\n".join(lines)
Tracking cost impact of completed actions

The value of team cost reviews is only visible if the impact of closed action items is measured. After an action item is marked complete, the next monthly review should include a retrospective delta: did unit cost improve as expected?

Publish impact measurements in the following month's pre-read under a 'Prior Quarter Impact' section. Teams that consistently achieve impact targets earn greater autonomy in budget management; teams with repeated misses receive additional FinOps support.

Teams sometimes push back against cost review findings when the data conflicts with their priorities. Engineers may argue that cost savings conflict with latency or quality goals. The FinOps practitioner's role is to make the trade-off explicit, not to make the decision.
Quantify the trade-off: 'This model switch saves $18K/month but increases p95 latency by 40ms — is that acceptable for this user-facing endpoint?'
Separate the decision from the meeting: if a trade-off requires more investigation, assign a spike with a one-week deadline rather than blocking the meeting.
Escalate to product leadership when cost and quality goals are irreconcilable at the team level — this is a product decision, not a FinOps decision.
Document all trade-off decisions with their rationale in the action log so that future reviews can revisit them if the situation changes.

The weekly check-in keeps the team aware of cost trends without scheduling overhead. It is delivered as a Slack message generated automatically from the cost pipeline — no human should manually compile it. Engineers read it in the thread and comment only when there is something to flag.

Automate this message with a scheduled job (cron or GitHub Actions) that runs every Monday at 9 AM. Store the rendered text in a Notion or Confluence page alongside the Slack post so that audit trails remain accessible after Slack's message history window closes.

The quarterly deep dive is the most consequential review cadence. It feeds into commitment renewals, headcount planning, and roadmap cost modelling. Because it involves finance and senior leadership, preparation must be more rigorous than the monthly review.
Quarter summary: spend vs forecast vs budget
Unit economics retrospective: cost/conv trend, AI margin
Updated unit cost targets for next quarter
Commitment utilisation review: pace, true-up risk
Roadmap cost modelling: upcoming features, model upgrades
Send a pre-read pack 72 hours before the quarterly review (not 48 hours like monthly) — it contains the unit economics retrospective and a first-draft forecast for sign-off.
Record the quarterly review (with consent) and post the recording plus minutes to the FinOps Confluence space within 24 hours.
The FinOps analyst owns the deck; the engineering lead owns the roadmap cost modelling slides — no single person should own the entire preparation.
Integrating cost reviews with sprint planning

Cost reviews are most effective when they connect directly to the engineering planning cycle. A simple integration: reserve one sprint-planning slot each month for a 10-minute 'FinOps story grooming' session where cost action items are converted into sprint stories with story points and acceptance criteria.

@dataclass
class SprintFinOpsStory:
    action_item_id: str
    title: str
    acceptance_criteria: list[str]
    story_points: int
    sprint: str   # e.g. "2025-Q3-S2"
    assigned_to: str

    def format_ticket_description(self) -> str:
        ac_lines = "\n".join(f"  - [ ] {c}" for c in self.acceptance_criteria)
        return (
            f"**FinOps Action Item: {self.action_item_id}**\n\n"
            f"{self.title}\n\n"
            f"**Acceptance Criteria:**\n{ac_lines}\n\n"
            f"**Story Points:** {self.story_points}\n"
            f"**Sprint:** {self.sprint}\n"
            f"**Assigned to:** {self.assigned_to}"
        )


# Example conversion
action = CostActionItem(
    id="CA-047",
    title="Switch summarisation service to Claude Haiku for short inputs",
    description="Inputs <500 tokens do not require Sonnet quality; Haiku costs 20x less",
    owner="alice@example.com",
    due_date=datetime.date(2025, 8, 15),
    success_metric="Reduce summarisation cost/call by 60% within 30 days",
    ticket_id="ENG-1234",
)

story = SprintFinOpsStory(
    action_item_id=action.id,
    title=action.title,
    acceptance_criteria=[
        "Model selection logic routes inputs <500 tokens to Haiku",
        "Unit tests cover both routing paths",
        "Cost/call metric drops by ≥60% in staging load test",
        "No increase in p95 latency for short inputs",
    ],
    story_points=3,
    sprint="2025-Q3-S2",
    assigned_to="alice@example.com",
)
print(story.format_ticket_description())
No pre-read distribution: meetings that open with data presentations waste the first 20 minutes and leave no time for decisions.
Action items assigned to teams rather than individuals: 'the team will investigate' guarantees no one investigates.
Skipping impact measurement: without measuring outcomes, teams have no signal about which types of interventions actually reduce cost.
Over-cadencing: weekly 45-minute reviews burn out engineers without improving outcomes — reserve depth for monthly meetings.
Four cadences serve different purposes: weekly async check-ins, monthly structured reviews, quarterly deep dives, and annual planning.
Every action item needs owner, due date, success metric, and ticket ID — any field missing means the item will not get done.
The pre-read report distributed 48 hours before the meeting is what allows decisions to happen rather than data presentations.
Impact measurement closes the loop: without it, teams cannot learn which interventions work and which are noise.
Trade-offs between cost, latency, and quality belong to product leadership — FinOps practitioners make the trade-off quantifiable, not the decision.
